frameworks.name: curriculum learning
frameworks.one_line: sequencing of task components to promote effective skill acquisition

frameworks.name: concept discovery vs concept implementation
frameworks.one_line: two graduable axes of complexity contrasted as curriculum strategies

core_question: When acquiring a complex task, which axis of complexity — discovering new concepts or implementing known concepts — should a curriculum graduate to maximize learning?
key_assumptions: complex task mastery decomposes into concept discovery and concept implementation
video-game tasks with sequential tool/ingredient use index both axes independently
self-directed curriculum choice reveals learner preferences but not necessarily optimal strategy
manipulations: graduation of concept discovery complexity (number of concepts)
graduation of concept implementation complexity (number of steps to goal)
key_findings: self-directed learners preferred curricula that graduated concept discovery complexity
learners assigned to graduate concept implementation complexity performed better
three randomized experiments confirmed implementation-graduated curricula outperform discovery-graduated curricula
interpretations: graduating concept implementation, not discovery, is the more effective curriculum strategy
learners should consolidate mastered concepts before attempting to discover new ones
